release-date.comment: Kickstarter funded 24 July 2017; board generally available from October 2017. LoveRPi first-look post is dated January 2018.

status.comment: Still actively sold by Libre Computer and major resellers (LoveRPi, Amazon) as of 2026-04-26. The newer Sweet Potato (AML-S905X-CC-V2) is a refresh, not a replacement.

open-source-hardware.comment: Libre Computer publishes schematics and 2D silkscreen plus 3D STEP/DXF/DWG models, but not full PCB design files (Gerber/KiCad/Altium) under an OSHW-compatible license. Per RESEARCH.md guidelines, schematics-only does not qualify as OSHW.

country-of-origin.comment: Designed by Shenzhen Libre Technology Co., Ltd., headquartered in Shenzhen, Guangdong, China.

process-node.comment: Amlogic S905X is fabricated on a 28 nm HPC process; Amlogic does not publish a definitive datasheet so this is from secondary coverage.

gpu.comment: BayLibre's mainline talk identifies a Mali-450 MP3 configuration; some marketing describes it as a 5-core (penta) Mali-450 counting the GP plus three PPs.

opengl-es-version.comment: Mali-450 (Utgard architecture) supports OpenGL ES 1.1 and 2.0 only; no OpenGL ES 3.x.

vulkan-support.comment: Mali-450 is too old to support Vulkan; no Vulkan driver exists in mainline (Lima) or vendor blobs.

hw-video-decode.comment: Vendor advertises 4K@60 hardware decode of H.264, H.265 (HEVC) and VP9. Mainline Linux support for HEVC/VP9 lagged the vendor kernel for years; H.264 and VP9 are upstream, HEVC merged later.

hw-video-encode.comment: S905X exposes only H.264 hardware encode; no advertised HEVC/VP9 encode.

ram-max-gb.comment: Sold in 1 GB and 2 GB SKUs only.

ram-type.comment: LoveRPi's hardware overview states the RAM runs at DDR4 speeds (2133 MHz). The S905X memory controller supports DDR3/DDR4/LPDDR3 — the Le Potato uses DDR4 (non-LP). RPi 3B+, by contrast, uses LPDDR2.

emmc.comment: eMMC connector on the reverse side; supports 5.x HS400 modules up to 128 GB. Module is sold separately.

ethernet.comment: 10/100 Mbit Fast Ethernet (RJ45). Not Gigabit despite some retailer listings claiming otherwise.

wifi.comment: No onboard Wi-Fi; SDIO Wi-Fi can be added via add-on or USB dongle.

usb-a-2-ports.comment: Four USB-A 2.0 ports; one nearest the Ethernet has its own bandwidth, the other three share.

usb-c-ports-data.comment: Power input is micro-USB, not USB-C.

poe-support.comment: Le Potato has 100M Ethernet only, no PoE support. The newer Sweet Potato (CC-V2) adds PoE; Le Potato itself does not.

max-display-resolution.comment: HDMI 2.0 with HDR; 4K@60 supported through the vendor kernel. Mainline kernel currently caps at 1080p60 per BayLibre.

audio-jack.comment: Combined CVBS composite/analog stereo audio jack (similar layout to the RPi 3 4-pole jack).

rpi-compatible-header.comment: Header is in the same physical position as RPi 3's 40-pin GPIO, but the pin assignments are NOT identical — Libre Computer ships a 'wiring tool' to translate Pi pin numbers. Hardware SPI does match RPi 2/3/4 on the same pin numbers, but the rest of the pinout differs.

rpi-hat-compatible.comment: Mechanically the board matches the RPi 3 footprint and case dimensions, but HATs that depend on specific RPi pin assignments (I2S audio, PoE, displays, etc.) will not work without remapping. No HAT EEPROM auto-config.

pcie-lanes-exposed.comment: Amlogic S905X does not expose PCIe.

i2c-spi-uart.comment: I²C, SPI, and UART (up to 921600 baud) are exposed on the 40-pin header.

power-input.comment: Micro-USB power input rated 5V (≥1.5A recommended; 5V/2A typical). No USB-C, no DC barrel jack.

idle-power-w.comment: Linux idle without HDMI = 0.8 W; idle with HDMI display attached = 1.0 W. Measured by vendor.

load-power-w.comment: Linux CPU burn (4× cores) plus HDMI display = 4.0 W (0.80 A at 5 V). Roughly half the equivalent figure for an RPi 4.

cooling-required.comment: Vendor sells optional heatsink kit but does not require active cooling at stock 1.5 GHz; sustained 4 W load is well within passive limits.

rtc-battery-header.comment: No onboard RTC battery holder; RTC requires external add-on via I²C.

length-mm.comment: Same external footprint as the Raspberry Pi 3 (86.9 × 58.5 × 19.1 mm).

form-factor-class.comment: PCB is within ~2 mm of 85×56 mm and the mounting holes match the RPi 3 pattern, so case compatibility is genuine even though the GPIO pinout is not.

weight-g.comment: Vendor does not publish bare-board weight, and reviewers haven't measured it. Amazon's package weight (~59 g) includes packaging and bundled accessories so cannot be used.

mainline-linux-kernel.comment: Le Potato (libretech-cc) DTS has been in mainline since kernel 4.14. CPU, microSD, eMMC, USB, Ethernet, HDMI (1080p60), and GPIO all work on a stock upstream kernel; Mali-450 is supported by the Lima driver and H.264/VP9 hardware decode are upstream. HEVC decode and 4K HDMI modes still benefit from the vendor kernel — see comment, but the board satisfies the 'full' threshold (Ethernet + USB + HDMI + GPIO on stock mainline).

official-os-image.comment: Libre Computer publishes first-party Debian, Raspbian, and Ubuntu images for the Le Potato, with active release notes.

distros-with-images.comment: First-party (Libre Computer): Debian 11/12, Raspbian/RPi OS 10/11/12, Ubuntu 20.04/22.04/24.04. Third-party with first-class support: Armbian (board officially maintained by core dev). LibreELEC, CoreELEC, and Lakka also publish images but are media/retro-gaming distros not on the schema's tag list.

bootloader.comment: Mainline U-Boot, chained from Amlogic ARM Trusted Firmware (BL30/BL31/BL32) blobs. Vendor images use U-Boot 2019.04+.

long-term-support.comment: Board is over 8 years old and still receives current-distro images (Ubuntu 24.04, Debian 12, RPi OS 12) from the vendor in 2026. Libre Computer's track record on this SKU comfortably exceeds 5 years of software updates.

android-support.comment: Android 9 / Android TV preview images have been published by Libre Computer; not refreshed to recent Android versions, but a maintained image exists.

msrp-base-usd.comment: Launch MSRP for the 2 GB SKU was $35; 1 GB sometimes sold lower. As of 2026-04-26, retail through LoveRPi/Amazon is roughly $30–$60 depending on bundle and availability.

msrp-top-usd.comment: Only one bare-board configuration (2 GB) at MSRP; 1 GB SKU is the same or lower price. Bundles (with eMMC, heatsink, Wi-Fi dongle) cost more but are accessories rather than higher-tier configurations.

available-regions.comment: Sold via Amazon (US, EU), LoveRPi (US), and various AliExpress / European resellers; not formally distributed in India through official channels.

typical-lead-time.comment: Snapshot 2026-04-26: in stock at LoveRPi and Amazon.

documentation-quality.comment: Adequate: official getting-started, GPIO pinout maps, schematics PDF, and first-party OS image release notes are all available on hub.libre.computer. Falls short of the Raspberry Pi standard (no full datasheet, no HAT spec, much of the deep info is community-contributed in forum threads).

community-size.comment: Active vendor hub plus a dedicated Armbian sub-forum, but daily volume is far below the RPi/Orange Pi/ODROID communities.

active-forum-discord.comment: Vendor-run Discourse hub (hub.libre.computer) with regular activity from Libre Computer staff and community.

accessory-ecosystem.comment: Cases (RPi 3 cases mostly fit), heatsinks, optional Wi-Fi dongles, eMMC modules, and a small selection of Libre Computer-branded add-ons. Generic 40-pin HATs only partially work due to the non-RPi pinout. Vibrant accessory ecosystem comparable to RPi/Orange Pi is absent.
